title: Going Places by A. R. Barton
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Fantasy versus reality, adolescent dreams, class barriers, and escapism.

summary:
“Going Places” is about Sophie, a teenage girl from a poor working-class family who escapes her dull reality through fantasies. She lives with her parents, her practical brother Geoff, and little Derek. Unlike her friend Jansie, who accepts that they are destined for the biscuit factory, Sophie dreams big. She wants to own a boutique, be an actress, or become a fashion designer.

Sophie idolizes her silent, secretive brother Geoff, who works as a mechanic and travels to places she can only imagine. To impress him, she invents a story that she met Danny Casey, a young Irish footballer she hero-worships, in the arcade. She says he asked for her autograph and promised to meet her again. Geoff believes her, but Jansie spreads the story. Sophie’s father dismisses it as another wild story and warns her not to dream beyond her class.

But Sophie convinces herself that the story is true. She dresses up and waits for Danny by the canal on Saturday, imagining every detail: his green eyes, soft voice, and the way he would come through the crowd. She waits till evening, but he never shows up. Though heartbroken, she still cannot accept reality. She walks home imagining what she will tell Geoff and telling herself Danny will come next week.

The title is ironic. Sophie dreams of “going places” in life, but she only goes to wait for a fantasy. The story shows the pain of adolescence, the gap between dreams and reality, and how hero-worship helps poor teenagers cope with limited futures.

qa.q: Where was it most likely that the two girls would find work after school?
qa.a: Sophie and Jansie were most likely to work in the biscuit factory after school.

qa.q: What were the options that Sophie was dreaming of? Why does Jansie discourage her from having such dreams?
qa.a: Sophie dreamed of opening a boutique, becoming a manager, an actress, or a fashion designer. Jansie discouraged her because she knew they belonged to a poor background and such dreams needed money and opportunity.

qa.q: Why did Sophie wriggle when Geoff told her father that she had met Danny Casey?
qa.a: Sophie wriggled because she felt nervous and uncomfortable. She knew her father would not believe her story and might scold her.

qa.q: Does Geoff believe what Sophie says about her meeting with Danny Casey?
qa.a: No, Geoff does not really believe her. He finds the story very unlikely, though he would like to believe it.

qa.q: Does her father believe her story?
qa.a: No, her father does not believe her story. He calls it another of her wild stories.

qa.q: How does Sophie include her brother Geoff in her fantasy of her future?
qa.a: Sophie imagines Geoff as part of her future world. She dreams that he will take her to unknown places on his motorcycle and help her enter a larger, more exciting world.

qa.q: Which country did Danny Casey play for?
Why didn’t Sophie want Jansie to know about her story with Danny?
qa.a: Sophie did not want Jansie to know because Jansie was talkative and Sophie feared that the whole neighbourhood would come to know about it.

qa.q: Did Sophie really meet Danny Casey?
qa.a: No, Sophie did not really meet Danny Casey. It was only her fantasy.

qa.q: Which was the only occasion when she got to see Danny Casey in person?
qa.a: She saw Danny Casey in person only during the football match when she went with her father and little Derek to watch United play.

qa.q: Sophie and Jansie were classmates and friends. What were the differences between them that show up in the story?
qa.a: Sophie was dreamy, imaginative, and ambitious. She wanted a glamorous future and often lived in fantasies. Jansie was practical and realistic. She understood their social and financial condition and knew that they would probably work in the biscuit factory.

qa.q: How would you describe the character and temperament of Sophie’s father?
qa.a: Sophie’s father was a hard-working, rough, and practical man. He was not gentle in speech and did not believe Sophie’s wild stories. He liked football and enjoyed going to the pub after a match.

qa.q: Why did Sophie like her brother Geoff more than any other person? From her perspective, what did he symbolise?
qa.a: Sophie liked Geoff because he was quiet and mysterious. She felt he knew a world outside her reach. For Sophie, Geoff symbolised freedom, adventure, and access to the unknown world she dreamed of.

qa.q: What socio-economic background did Sophie belong to? What are the indicators of her family’s financial status?
qa.a: Sophie belonged to a lower-middle-class or working-class family. The indicators are: she and Jansie were expected to work in a biscuit factory, her house was small and cluttered, her father was a labouring man, her brother was an apprentice mechanic, and her family could not afford her dreams of a boutique or a fashionable career.
